title: Expanding Managed Agents in Gemini API:  background tasks, remote MCP and more
gist: Google announces new capabilities for Managed Agents in the Gemini Interactions API — background/async execution, remote MCP server integration, custom function calling alongside sandbox tools, and network credential refresh — positioning managed agents as asynchronous workers that run reasoning, code execution and file management inside an isolated cloud sandbox via a single endpoint.

themes.theme: Managed agents as a single-endpoint sandbox

insights.claim: With managed agents in the Gemini Interactions API, a developer calls a single endpoint and Gemini handles reasoning, code execution, package installation, file management and web information inside an isolated cloud sandbox.
insights.speaker: Philipp Schmid
insights.so_what: Abstracting the full agent runtime behind one endpoint lowers the barrier to production agents and deepens Gemini platform lock-in.
insights.quote: you call a single endpoint and Gemini handles reasoning, code execution, package installation, file management and web information inside an isolated cloud sandbox.
insights.locator: Expanding Managed Agents in Gemini API

insights.claim: Google frames the updates as turning managed agents into asynchronous workers that operate inside real development environments without blocking the calling application.
insights.speaker: Philipp Schmid
insights.so_what: Positioning agents as durable async workers targets long-running enterprise automation, competing with agent frameworks that require self-hosted orchestration.
insights.quote: These updates turn managed agents into asynchronous workers that operate inside real development environments without blocking your application.

insights.claim: Passing background: true runs Gemini interactions asynchronously on the server and immediately returns an ID that clients use to poll for status, stream progress, or reconnect later while the agent finishes remotely.
insights.speaker: Philipp Schmid
insights.so_what: Server-side async execution removes the fragility of holding HTTP connections open, a prerequisite for reliable long-running agent tasks.
insights.quote: Holding an HTTP connection open for long-running tasks is fragile. Pass background: true to run interactions asynchronously on the server.

insights.claim: Managed agents can now connect directly to remote Model Context Protocol servers by passing an mcp_server tool at interaction time, letting the agent reach private databases or internal APIs from its secure sandbox without custom proxy middleware.
insights.speaker: Philipp Schmid
insights.so_what: Native remote MCP support makes Gemini agents a drop-in orchestrator for enterprise internal tools, expanding addressable use cases.
insights.quote: you can now connect managed agents directly to remote Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers.

insights.claim: Developers can mix and match remote MCP tools with built-in sandbox capabilities like Google Search or code execution within a single interaction.
insights.speaker: Philipp Schmid
insights.so_what: Combining internal tools with built-in search and code execution in one call reduces integration overhead for composite enterprise workflows.
insights.quote: You can mix and match remote tools with built-in sandbox capabilities.

themes.theme: Custom function calling with step matching

insights.claim: Custom functions can run alongside built-in sandbox tools using step matching: built-in tools run automatically on the server while custom functions transition the interaction to requires_action so the client executes local business logic.
insights.speaker: Philipp Schmid
insights.so_what: The requires_action pattern cleanly separates server-side and client-side execution, key for embedding proprietary logic in managed agents.
insights.quote: Built-in tools will run automatically on the server, while custom functions transition the interaction to requires_action so your client executes local business logic.
insights.locator: Custom function calling alongside sandbox tools

insights.claim: Developers can refresh expired credentials or rotate keys by passing an existing environment_id with a new network configuration on the next interaction, and the new rules replace the old ones immediately.
insights.speaker: Philipp Schmid
insights.so_what: In-place credential rotation without losing session state solves a practical blocker for long-lived agents accessing authenticated services.
insights.quote: You can refresh credentials or rotate keys by passing your existing environment_id with a new network configuration on your next interaction. The new rules replace the old ones immediately.

insights.claim: A refreshed sandbox environment keeps its filesystem state, installed packages and cloned repositories intact across interactions.
insights.speaker: Philipp Schmid
insights.so_what: Persistent sandbox state enables multi-step, stateful agent workflows that would otherwise require rebuilding the environment each call.
insights.quote: Your sandbox keeps its filesystem state, installed packages and cloned repositories intact.
